problem_summary: 2023 MCM/ICM Problem F (policy) on Green GDP: the team had to (1) select an existing GGDP calculation method that, if substituted for GDP, would have a measurable effect on climate change; (2) build a model estimating the global climate-mitigation impact of adopting GGDP as the primary indicator of economic health; (3) weigh the benefits (climate mitigation) against the costs/disadvantages of the switch to judge whether it is worthwhile; (4) do an in-depth analysis of one country's behavior/condition after GGDP adoption (they chose Brunei, an oil-dependent state); and (5) write a one-page non-technical report to that country's leader. The team organized the entire response around a memorable "4M System" — Method, Model, Mould, Movement — mapping one deliverable to each task.
models_used: GGDP calculation formula adopted from Stjepanovic et al. (2017/2019): GGDP = GDP - (KtCO2 x PCDM) - (Twaste x 74kWh x Pelect) - (GNI/100 x %NRD), subtracting carbon-pollution cost, waste opportunity cost, and natural-resource depletion
Derived diagnostic metric %DIF = |GDP-GGDP|/GDP x 100 ('unhealthy share' of GDP) used as the single driver variable threaded through every later model

Entropy Weight Method (EWM) to objectively weight 21 third-level climate indicators (atmosphere/ocean/land), with attribute-type-specific min-max normalization for benefit/cost/interval/optimal attributes, producing a composite climate score S = 100 * sum(p_ij * w_j)
Pearson correlation analysis (correlation matrix) over 11 secondary indicators to prune redundant variables before regression
Multiple linear regression of climate indicators (GHGE, WT, pH, SSTA, Sea Level) on GDP and %DIF, yielding the headline finding that %DIF accounts for ~1/3 of climate change
Cost-benefit analysis framed via intergenerational equity and regional equity (costs) vs sustainable development, economic growth, moderate change (benefits)
Ordinary Least Squares (OLS) regression of four social-welfare indicators (GII, II, HDI, TDB) separately on GDP and GGDP to show welfare effects of the switch

Re-parameterized GGDP forecasting model GGDP = EC/ECPGDP - %DIF x GDP, with log-linear OLS trend fits for ln(EC) and ln(ECPGDP) and an exponential model %DIF = e^a e^(bt); a delta=5% capacity-reduction shock distinguishes reform vs no-reform scenarios

Three competing time-series forecasters for the climate score — ARIMA, Grey Model GM(1,1), and Support Vector Machine (SVM/'SVG') — then selected GM(1,1) by rejecting ARIMA (too pessimistic) and SVM (too flat)
Logistic function normalization y = 1/(1+exp(-b(x-x0))) to bound the unbounded climate score into [0,100] and handle negative forecasts

Country case study (Brunei): resource-depletion timeline from proven oil reserves / annual production (~85 years), Resource-Dependence-Rate-based recovery-time mapping, and a staged policy roadmap (policy/trading/industrial/economic structure)
key_techniques: Reducing a multi-dimensional policy question to one scalar driver (%DIF) so that climate, welfare, and economic models can all be regressed against the same variable, giving a unified causal story
Using EWM for objective, data-driven weights instead of subjective AHP/expert weighting — a defensible choice for 21 heterogeneous indicators
Ensemble-then-select forecasting: running ARIMA + GM(1,1) + SVM in parallel and openly rejecting two on physical-plausibility grounds rather than silently picking one

Logistic normalization specifically introduced to fix the failure mode of raw forecasts going negative (which violated the score's definition) — turning a modeling problem into a methodological strength

Scenario contrast (reform vs no-reform) driven by a single tunable shock parameter delta, enabling clean before/after comparison and a natural 'intersection/recovery year' narrative (2052/2055 world, 2077 Brunei)
Correlation-screening before regression to justify variable selection and avoid multicollinearity
Quantifying a headline soundbite ('%DIF is ~1/3 of climate change') that judges and the non-technical reader can remember

Sensitivity analysis via 3%/5%/10% perturbations of Energy Consumption, used not just to show robustness but to surface a counter-intuitive policy insight (bigger short-term sacrifice => faster recovery)
why_it_won: "The paper reads as a single coherent argument rather than five disconnected task answers. The '4M System' (Method/Model/Mould/Movement) gives judges an instantly graspable scaffold that maps 1:1 onto the COMAP tasks, signaling completeness at a glance. It satisfies the literal problem requirement (Task 1 explicitly asks to *choose an existing* GGDP method) by adopting and properly citing the Stjepanovic formula instead of inventing one, then adds original value by deriving %DIF and proving — with real-data regression — that this single variable explains about one third of climate change. That quantified, memorable claim is exactly the kind of decision-relevant result a policy problem rewards. The team closes the full loop the prompt demands: global impact -> worthwhile? (cost-benefit + OLS welfare) -> single-country deep dive (Brunei, a genuinely well-chosen extreme case because oil is >60% of its GDP) -> an actual one-page letter to the King. The forecasting section shows modeling maturity (three methods, honest rejection of two), the logistic fix shows they noticed and repaired their own failure, and the sensitivity analysis produces a non-obvious policy takeaway. Heavy, purposeful visualization (work flowchart, indicator tree, world heatmaps, recovery-time map, timeline) lets a judge reconstruct the whole pipeline from figures alone."
section_highlights: "Summary sheet: opens with the 4M mnemonic and front-loads concrete numbers (%DIF fell ~5%, %DIF is 1/3 of climate change, recovery in 2052/2055/2077) so judges see results before methods. Section 4 (Method): a clean three-category literature taxonomy of GGDP methods (revise-GDP / replace-GDP / supplement-GDP) that justifies the chosen formula and demonstrates the required literature engagement. Section 5 (Model): the EWM indicator hierarchy (3 first-level, 11 second-level, 21 third-level) plus correlation-matrix pruning into a focused 5-variable regression — a textbook 'narrow from many indicators to a few drivers' move. Section 6 (Mould): cost-benefit organized into named, quotable buckets (intergenerational/regional equity vs sustainable development/economic growth/moderate change) and the GDP-vs-GGDP OLS matrix that honestly flags income inequality slightly *worsening*. Section 7 (Movement): Brunei chosen precisely because it is the hardest case, with a concrete resource-exhaustion timeline and a four-part policy roadmap figure. Section 8: sensitivity analysis that yields the memorable 'bigger sacrifice => faster recovery' insight. Section 10: a genuinely non-technical, plainly-worded letter to the King that translates 2077 recovery into 'temporary sacrifices for longer-term happiness.'"
pitfalls_or_limitations: The headline claim '%DIF is ~1/3 of climate change' is asserted from regression coefficients compared loosely against UNEP rates (0.2C/yr, 3.1mm/yr); the derivation is hand-wavy and the causal interpretation of a coefficient as a fraction of 'climate change' is not rigorously defined

Multiple regressions report suspiciously high R-squared (0.89-0.98) on time-trended macro series — almost certainly spurious regression from shared time trends and autocorrelation; no stationarity/Durbin-Watson checks are shown

The beta_1 (GDP) coefficients in the climate regressions are ~1e-12 to 1e-15, effectively zero, so the model essentially regresses on %DIF alone — the 'GDP and %DIF' framing overstates what was actually fit
GM(1,1) is chosen over ARIMA/SVM largely because its output 'looks right' (not too pessimistic, not too flat), which is selection-by-eyeball rather than by validation error
Forecasts run 80+ years to 2100+ from short historical series; the paper admits long-horizon deviation but still presents precise recovery years (2052/2055/2077) as if reliable
Strong simplifying assumptions (no wars/pandemics, no major climate shocks, all leaders rational and compliant) remove exactly the political-economy frictions a policy problem should grapple with
OCR/figure data in the rendered tables is partly garbled (e.g. duplicated heatmap rows, placeholder-filled line charts), so several figures cannot be independently verified from the artifact
delta=5% annual capacity reduction is asserted, not estimated; the entire reform scenario hinges on this single unjustified parameter
Welfare conclusion rests on only four indicators (GII, II, HDI, TDB), which the team itself flags as non-comprehensive
reusable_patterns: The 'one Method / one Model / one Mould / one Movement' (or any 4-noun mnemonic) scaffold to map deliverables 1:1 onto the official tasks and make completeness legible to judges
Derive a single scalar 'driver' variable early (here %DIF) and thread it through every downstream model so the whole paper tells one causal story instead of five separate ones

For 'choose an existing method' tasks: present a short literature taxonomy (categorize approaches into 3 buckets), then justify your pick — this satisfies the literal requirement and shows research depth
EWM + attribute-type-aware normalization as a reusable, defensible objective-weighting pipeline whenever you must collapse 10-30 heterogeneous indicators into one score
Correlation-matrix screening to prune indicators down to a small regression-ready set, with the matrix shown as justification
Ensemble-and-reject forecasting: run ARIMA + GM(1,1) + SVM, then explicitly reject methods on physical-plausibility grounds — demonstrates judgment, not just tool use
Logistic-function bounding to keep an unbounded score in [0,100] and gracefully handle forecasts that would otherwise go negative
Single-parameter scenario shock (reform vs no-reform via delta) to generate a clean before/after 'recovery/intersection year' narrative
Pick the hardest/most extreme single-country case (Brunei, >60% oil) so the deep-dive showcases the model under stress and yields a vivid story
Mine the sensitivity analysis for a counter-intuitive policy insight ('bigger short-term sacrifice => faster recovery') rather than just asserting robustness
Write the non-technical report in genuine plain language with one concrete, emotionally framed takeaway ('temporary sacrifices for longer-term happiness') and a specific year the reader can hold onto
